data.title: Ford Motor: Solid EV Momentum
data.description:
The stock price of Ford Motor Company (NYSE:F) has been hovering around $10 pretty much since August 2024 though the auto company is growing its EV sales and inflation has been moderating quite substantially, leading to profit upside.

While high inflation was a considerable challenge for U.S. auto manufacturers to overcome in 2023 and 2024, primarily because of higher materials and labor costs, retreating inflation creates a catalyst for profit growth, as does solid momentum in terms of EV sales.

In my last stock classification for Ford Motor, I pointed to the auto company’s 6% yield as one reason why investors would want to gobble up a few shares for their passive income portfolios. The scaling of electric-vehicle deliveries, in a low-inflation environment, is now one possible catalyst for accelerating profit growth for Ford Motor and estimates for 2026 are looking quite good.

Improving Model E EBIT Margin Trend, Narrowing Of EV Unit Losses

Electric vehicles sales have not been growing as fast as anticipated, with consumers slowing their rate of purchases, particularly in 2024, amid challenges to consumer spending.

High inflation and trade uncertainty have weighed on consumers’ willingness to make large purchases, such as new pickup trucks or SUVs. That being said, Ford Motor is still seeing quite robust growth in its electric-vehicle division, which is going to remain key for the auto company to produce some healthy profit growth moving forward.

Inflation has seen a rapid downturn so far in the last couple of months, with data showing a third consecutive monthly decline in the pace of inflation this year. Inflation in April stood at an annualized 2.3% and inflation cooled particularly quickly in both March and April. For U.S. auto companies like Ford Motor, falling inflation and rising EV sales, could mean that the profit outlook is improving.

In 2025, all eyes should be on Ford Motor’s electric-vehicle division, which is still racking up a good amount of losses, but the auto company is some considerable momentum here.

In the first quarter, Ford Motor’s electric-vehicle unit produced an EBIT loss of $0.8 billion, which may not be great news for investors as a loss is still a loss, and quite a substantial one at that. That said, however, losses decreased by a whopping $0.6 billion QoQ and I think that if Ford Motor maintains its present delivery momentum, the auto company has a good shot at outperforming its $7.0-8.5 billion EBIT target for 2025.

EBIT margins in Ford Motor’s electric-vehicle segment are improving at a quick pace which comes on the back of higher deliveries and the starting effects of economies of scale.

Still, Ford Model e weighs on Ford’s total EBIT margins, but as the company scales its delivery volumes and launches higher margin products, I think the company could potentially surprise in terms of narrowing its segment losses this year.

Though it is true that electric-vehicle sales momentum has slowed a bit in 2024, it is clear from industry data, as well as Ford Motor’s reports, that the auto company is growing its deliveries at a remarkable pace.

In 1Q25, Ford Motor’s EV sales increased 12% YoY, but the auto company compares particularly favorably against Tesla Inc. (TSLA) which exhibited a decrease in their sales volumes of 9% YoY during that same quarter.

Tesla has been harshly punished by consumers in the U.S. and Europe this year, which is partially attributable to his acting as a high-profile advisor to President Trump. Since Elon Musk has since publicly dialed back his exposure to partisan politics, I think that Tesla’s stock is still very much compelling as a long-term EV investment, despite its sky-high valuation multiple.

Though it is true that electric-vehicle sales momentum has slowed a bit in 2024, it is clear from industry data, as well as Ford Motor’s reports, that the auto company is growing its deliveries at a remarkable pace. In 1Q25, Ford Motor’s EV sales increased 12% YoY, but the auto company compares particularly favorably against Tesla Inc. (TSLA) which exhibited a decrease in their sales volumes of 9% YoY during that same quarter.

Tesla has been harshly punished by consumers in the U.S. and Europe this year, which is partially attributable to his acting as a high-profile advisor to President Trump. Since Elon Musk has since publicly dialed back his exposure to partisan politics, I think that Tesla’s stock is still very much compelling as a long-term EV investment, despite its sky-high valuation multiple.

Ford Motor’s Juicy 6% Yield Might Be Undervalued

If you are looking for a robust 6% dividend yield from an S&P 500 company, Ford Motor should be very high on your shopping or watch-list. Ford Motor is paying investors $0.15 per share per quarter, but has also paid special dividends in the past. In the last three years, Ford Motor paid special dividends varying in size (the most recent one of $0.15 per share) in March 2025.

Ford Motor’s valuation reflects a high safety margin as its stocks are changing hands for only 7.7x 2026e profits while the auto company is anticipated to grow its profits by 26%. Ford Motor’s arch-rival, General Motors Company (GM), is selling for 5.1x 2026 estimated profits, but Ford Motor is poised to outshine General Motors in terms of profit growth by a whopping 24 percentage points.

Pure EV-play Tesla is selling for 104.6x next year’s estimated profits and is expected to see 51% profit growth YoY thanks partially to the re-launch of a newer version of Tesla’s best-selling Model Y.

That said, many investors are not particularly eager to invest in Tesla right now, mainly because we don’t yet whether or not the consumer strike in 1Q25 will have a lasting effect. Tesla has also disappointed in recent years in terms of its delayed robotaxi.

From a yield angle, however, I think that Ford Motor is an exceptionally interesting stock; it presently pays passive income investors a solid 5.8% dividend yield and though the dividend is not growing, the yield itself is about 4.5 times higher than the S&P 500 yield.

In addition, EV momentum is still benefiting Ford Motor and with a potential acceleration of growth in the electric-vehicle segment, catalyzed by receding inflation and a boost to consumer spending, the auto company could even be putting stronger-than-anticipated results on the table in 2025.

Why The Investment Thesis Might Be, Against My Expectations, More Bearish

Ford Motor’s transition to electric vehicles is not going as smooth or as fast as the market predicted two or three years ago. Auto companies including Ford Motor and General Motors have scaled back their investment activities with regards to EV transitions because of slowing adoption of electric vehicles in the market, but the market is still growing, benefiting companies that made past investments in manufacturing capacity.

With Ford Motor’s EV sales still growing and the stock paying a handsome dividend, I think that the risk/reward relationship will ultimately benefit long-term investors.

My Takeaway

Ford Motor might offer passive income investors a better investment package than a first glance would indicate. The auto company is scaling up its electric-vehicle deliveries and its EV division losses are narrowing, which could set the auto company up for potential earnings surprises as well as stronger-than-anticipated EBIT.

Though the Model e segment is still very much anticipated to be a loss-leader for Ford Motor in 2025, I think that the context of moderating inflation and surging wholesale [EV] volumes is potentially creating a favorable setup for the auto company to accelerate its profit growth.

Importantly, the stock of Ford Motor is not particularly expensive, so its valuation reflects a high margin of safety already. Buy.

data.title: Municipals: Relative Value In U.S. Fixed Income
data.description:
By Kevin Flanagan

While uncertainty will continue to hover over the money and bond markets in the months ahead, the good news is that investors have been given a scenario where yield levels have returned to "normal" levels. This development has provided a "new" landscape to consider when putting money to work in the U.S. fixed arena. In my opinion, one sector that currently offers relative value is the municipal (muni) bond market.

Relative Value Ratio

Typically, when analyzing the muni market, one key gauge that is focused on is known as the "relative value ratio." This ratio is simply the percentage of the yield offered on a muni security as compared to a U.S. Treasury (UST) security, with the UST 10-Year being the traditional comparison. The general rule of thumb is that the higher this percentage is, the cheaper and better relative value munis will be. Remember, this is before taxes are taken into consideration…more on that aspect down below.

Muni Relative Value Ratios

The graph above outlines relative ratios over the last nearly 2 ½ years for the three key sectors of the muni market: AAA Callable, AA Revenue and A Revenue. As you can see, the ratios have been on the rise here in 2025, i.e., munis have underperformed the UST 10-Year. In terms of the ratios themselves, each one is about 10 percentage points above its average respective readings during this period.

In fact, as of this writing, municipals are the only major U.S. fixed income sector that has produced a negative return (-1.1%) thus far this year. Three things come to mind for the reason behind this underperformance: increased supply, seasonal selling during tax season and concerns about whether the "big, beautiful bill" would change the federal tax-exempt status of munis. The first two reasons tend to sort themselves out each year, but more importantly, the House-passed version kept this tax-exempt status intact, and it is highly likely the final version of the "bb" bill will as well.

Tax-Equivalent Yield (TEY)

That brings us to the tax-equivalent yield aspect of munis. The TEY enables investors to compare the yield on taxable and tax-exempt securities and represents the yield an investment in taxable bonds would have to earn in order to match, after federal income taxes, the yield available on an investment in tax-exempt municipal bonds.

WisdomTree recently launched two municipal Funds, the WisdomTree Core Laddered Municipal Fund (WTMU) and the WisdomTree High Income Laddered Municipal Fund (WTMY), in conjunction with industry experts Insight Investment. As their titles specify, these two Funds utilize the time-tested laddered approach to fixed income investing and are active solutions.

The TEY for a fund is based on the fund's SEC 30-day yield and uses the highest marginal federal income tax rate. State income and alternative minimum taxes are excluded from the calculation.

Tax-Equivalent Municipal SEC Yields vs. U.S. Fixed Income

The chart above clearly illustrates how the TEYs for both WTMU and WTMY are visibly above the U.S. investment-grade fixed income universe. An investor would need to move strictly into all high-yield corporates in order to find a higher-yielding instrument.

Conclusion

In my opinion, the municipal market appears to be telling investors that, besides the traditional tax-advantaged solution, munis could also be considered for broader fixed income portfolios.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including the possible loss of principal. Municipal securities carry various risks, including credit, interest rate, prepayment and valuation risks. Issuers may face financial difficulties that impact their ability to meet payment obligations. The value of these securities can fluctuate due to changes in revenue sources, local economic and political conditions and industry-specific downturns (e.g., education, health care, transportation, utilities). Additionally, tax-exempt income from municipal securities could become taxable due to regulatory changes or issuer noncompliance, potentially reducing their value. Fixed income investments are subject to interest rate risk; their value will normally decline as interest rates rise. Higher-yield securities or “junk” bonds have lower credit ratings and involve a greater risk to principal. Fixed income investments are also subject to credit risk, the risk that the issuer of a bond will fail to pay interest and principal in a timely manner or that negative perceptions of the issuer’s ability to make such payments will cause the price of that bond to decline. While the Fund attempts to limit credit and counterparty exposure, the value of an investment in the Fund may change quickly and without warning in response to issuer or counterparty defaults and changes in the credit ratings of the Fund’s portfolio investments. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Kevin Flanagan, Head of Fixed Income Strategy

As part of WisdomTree’s Investment Strategy group, Kevin serves as Head of Fixed Income Strategy. In this role, he contributes to the asset allocation team, writes fixed income-related content and travels with the sales team, conducting client-facing meetings and providing expertise on WisdomTree’s existing and future bond ETFs. In addition, Kevin works closely with the fixed income team. Prior to joining WisdomTree, Kevin spent 30 years at Morgan Stanley, where he was Managing Director and Chief Fixed Income Strategist for Wealth Management. He was responsible for tactical and strategic recommendations and created asset allocation models for fixed income securities. He was a contributor to the Morgan Stanley Wealth Management Global Investment Committee, primary author of Morgan Stanley Wealth Management’s monthly and weekly fixed income publications, and collaborated with the firm’s Research and Consulting Group Divisions to build ETF and fund manager asset allocation models. Kevin has an MBA from Pace University’s Lubin Graduate School of Business, and a B.S in Finance from Fairfield University.

data.title: Academy Sports and Outdoors: Staying Neutral Until There Is More Near-Term Outlook Clarity
data.description:
Investment overview

I give a hold rating for Academy Sports and Outdoors, Inc. (NASDAQ:ASO), as I am worried about the near-term outlook. Execution on store expansion, premium brand partnerships, and higher-income customer traction is encouraging. However, weakness among core lower-income consumers, inventory risks, and macro uncertainties create near-term uncertainties. I prefer to wait for more stable consumer trends or tariff clarity before getting more bullish.

Company Description

ASO is a sporting goods and outdoor recreation retailer in the US. As of 1Q25, it has 303 stores across 21 states. The business model is to offer value-driven products across outdoor, sports & recreation, apparel, and footwear categories. The distinctive aspect is that ASO focuses its product assortments with a mix of top national brands and exclusive private-label brands. This allows ASO to serve a broad range of customers—from casual families to dedicated athletes.

1Q25 Earnings Review

ASO 1Q25 results were mixed. Net sales fell 0.9% y/y to $1.35 billion, and comparable sales fell 3.7%. The poor sales performance was attributed to the weather, and management pointed out that trends have improved each month in the quarter, ending with positive comparable sales in April. Gross margin did well, though, expanding by 60 bps y/y to 34% on stronger merchandise margins and lower shrink. Unfortunately, that expansion was offset by higher operating costs. SG&A grew 10.2% y/y, as ASO spent on new-store openings, tech upgrades, and marketing around key brand launches. The net result was adj. EPS of $0.76.

Positive takeaway

I will start with the positive takeaway I have for ASO. Firstly, the store expansion plan is progressing very well. Shops opened in 2022 and 2023 are already posting low-single-digit comparable sales growth—which beats ASO consolidated store base performance. Management also noted that the 2024 class is off to a great start, which suggests this low-single-digits trend has sustained. This traction gives me confidence new units can drive top-line growth.

Secondly, traffic from households earning >$100k grew double digits in 1Q25, and it has been a trend over the last several quarters. This shows that ASO is benefiting directly from the trade-down motion, wherein higher-income customers are facing the heat from the poor macro backdrop and are looking for more value alternatives. This also has long-term growth implications. If ASO can take this opportunity to show this richer demographic that its products are not of inferior quality (just deemed as value choices), it could hold on to this market share over time.

Thirdly, the latest Nike launch (Jordan Brand in 145 stores in late April) could continue to be a near-term growth driver. These new products have been attracting a lot of heat, and we know this because despite being just in stores for 2 weeks of the quarter, the sales performance already exceeded management’s expectations, and the trend has sustained into May. This launch also has a broader implication on ASO business, as the launch of the Jordan brand raises ASO's profile with premium vendors and potentially opens the door to other popular brands.

Core Consumer and Macro Headwinds Remain

While ASO is gaining traction with its store expansion strategy and higher-income shoppers, there are issues that, I believe, may weigh heavily on the near-term stock sentiment. Firstly, store traffic from lower-income households (particularly those earning <$50,000) is declining. Remember that at ASO’s core, this is a value retailer, and the lower-income customer cohort is its key customer base. Also, management also pointed out cautious spending among Hispanic consumers, which is a key demographic for the company. These are not the signs that I like to see for a value retailer like ASO. If anything, this tells me that the negative impact of the ongoing trade-down motion (lower-income customers stop buying completely) has started to outweigh the positive impacts (higher-income customers trading down). This is exceptionally worrying when we consider that ASO is expanding its store base today. While this could help gain share over the long term, the near-term impact on earnings could be huge as ASO core customers continue to spend less (more stores = higher fixed costs).

Also, despite the Jordan launch and comparable sales trend picking up in April, May sales returned to a low-single-digit decline. This tells me that the demand remains very volatile and makes the near-term growth difficult to forecast. If we combine these sales concerns with the elevated inventory that ASO is carrying, it becomes more concerning. Inventory grew 15% y/y in 1Q25, which is significantly above the 0.9% sales decline. Some of these inventories ($85 million worth) are pulled forward ahead of potential tariff hikes, and while this may offer cost advantages if tariffs are imposed, it also increases the risk of future markdowns in a soft demand environment. If sales trends fail to improve, there is a good chance that ASO may be forced to discount these inventories, which could pressure margins and hurt profitability.

Valuation

For valuation, I don’t think the risk/reward is attractive enough to justify investors taking the risks I mentioned above. ASO is valued at ~7.3x NTM PE, and this is near the historical trading average over the past 5 years. It could really go both ways, in my opinion. If tariff risks go away, the US macro situation gets better with lower inflation and interest rates, and the tax bill passes, consumer demand could really pick up and drive demand for ASO. In this scenario, I wouldn’t be surprised to see the NTM PE go up to 9x (+1 standard deviation). However, the flip side is true as well. Based on the current situation, inflation is at risk of surging again due to tariffs, and rates are still high. Tariff negotiations are also uncertain. If things turn south, ASO could easily derate back to where it traded earlier this year at 6x NTM PE.

Conclusion

I give a hold rating for ASO. The company is executing well on expanding its store base, strengthening brand partnerships, and attracting more affluent shoppers. But these positives are offset by weakening demand from its core customer base, uncertain sales trends, and growing inventory in a softening environment. I think investors are better off staying on the sidelines for now until visibility improves.

data.title: U.S. Investor Risk Aversion Extends To 5th Straight Month In June
data.description:
Overall US investor sentiment leaned toward risk aversion for the fifth month in a row, even as caution in equity markets diminished for a third straight month.

The results of the monthly S&P Global Investment Manager Index US equity investor sentiment survey, released June 10, showed the Risk Appetite Index rose to negative 13% in June from negative 19% in May and negative 31% in April. Though sentiment has improved each month since a recent low point in March, the index in June continued its stretch of negative readings, indicating risk aversion that began in February. The index measures net risk appetite among surveyed investors, with those reporting a high tolerance or aversion counting with double weight.

Equity markets were volatile in March and April as the US readied new tariffs on nearly all major trading partners. Stocks have since rebounded from an April sell-off after most tariffs were delayed until July while global governments pursued trade negotiations with the US. Still, market caution persisted as negotiations played out and market observers pivoted their attention to a budget bill now being debated by the US Congress.

"While the survey has seen some calming of worries over tariffs, the political environment remains the biggest drag on equity performance," Chris Williamson, executive director at S&P Global Market Intelligence, said in a report released alongside the survey results. "Fiscal worries surrounding the deficit and cuts in spending in areas such as Medicare are also driving risk aversion, with little scope for any boost to equities meanwhile seen from US Federal Reserve rate cuts."

Near-term equity losses still expected

Even with the easing in risk aversion, investors expect net market losses in the coming month.

The survey's Equity Returns Index slipped to negative 32% in June from negative 29% in May. The score reflects the percentage of surveyed respondents expecting an improvement in equity returns over the 30 days following the end of the survey, minus the percentage of those expecting net equity losses. June's survey score indicated a slightly more pessimistic outlook than in May.

For the full year, investors anticipate a modest year-over-year gain for the S&P 500, with the median expectation projecting the index will close out the year at 6,000. This would be a 2% improvement from 2024, compared with the 23% surge from 2023 to 2024.

Surveyed respondents indicated that only shareholder returns were likely to contribute to positive market performance in June, when compared with seven other market-influencing factors. By contrast, most surveyed investors believed that the political environment, high stock valuations and government fiscal policy would place the most pressure on equity returns.

The index readings for political environment and stock valuations have consistently been the lowest this year among all market factors.

The survey index score of negative 33% in June for the US macroeconomic environment improved 29 percentage points from May. Though the outlook remained pessimistic, this was the biggest improvement in sentiment among all eight market factors.

IT emerges as most preferred sector

Investors signaled bullishness in six of 11 industries in June, with the greatest preference for information technology, according to the survey's sector index.

Sentiment toward the industrial and communication sectors flipped from negative in May to positive in June, with the communication sector showing as the second-most preferred sector overall this month.

The energy sector was reported to have the largest improvement in index score in June, with a 34-percentage-point increase month over month, though investor net outlook for the sector remained negative.

Sentiment for the healthcare sector was the only one to deteriorate to negative in June from positive in May. This is the first time in the survey's five-year history that investors reported bearishness in the sector. The survey report attributed the pessimistic outlook to concerns over potential Medicare cuts in the US.

The consumer discretionary sector was the most out of favor with investors in June, followed by real estate. The real estate sector's index score in June dropped the most month over month, with a 19-percentage-point decrease to negative 35%.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Intel: A Deep Dive Into The Turnaround Potential
data.description:
Intel Corporation (NASDAQ:INTC) (NEOE:INTC:CA) is a veteran in the chip sector, but the stock has had a rough few years. The price peaked at around $60 in 2021 and has since fallen to around $20. Over the past 5 years, the stock has lost more than half of its value, partly due to missed technological opportunities and fierce competition. Intel has recently been in the news again with the appointment of a new CEO, Lip-Bu Tan, in March 2025. This seasoned semiconductor veteran should give Intel new strength after a few difficult years. With the entire chip industry in the grip of AI and geopolitics, Intel is more relevant than ever as a potential turnaround story.

On the one hand, Intel is facing serious challenges. From market share loss to margin pressure but on the other hand, its current low valuation offers opportunities for contrarian investors. The stock recently took a hit of around 7% after disappointing guidance from Q2 despite a better than expected Q1 profit. I analyze Intel's prospects and assess whether it is worth buying INTC at these levels.

AI Is Booming, But Where Is Intel?

Intel’s future is strongly influenced by megatrends such as AI, the rise of the foundry market and geopolitical shifts. NVIDIA dominates with GPUs for machine learning, while Intel is still searching for its place. Intel does develop AI-specialized chips (such as Gaudi accelerators) and builds AI functionality into CPUs but lags behind NVIDIA’s leading position. This stark contrast became apparent last year when NVIDIA’s data center revenue exploded due to AI demand, while Intel mainly benefited from additional CPU sales for AI servers. However, AI also offers opportunities for Intel: hyperscalers need not only GPUs but also masses of CPUs to keep AI systems running, a role in which Intel is still the market leader. In addition, Intel is investing in software initiatives to better support AI workloads on Xeon processors.

Can Intel Become A Western Alternative To TSMC?

Another focus point is Intel’s entry as a foundry for external customers. This strategic shift must compete with TSMC, Samsung and GlobalFoundries. Intel is positioning itself as a Western alternative now that the US and Europe are rebuilding their semiconductor production (reshoring). The company promises to produce chips for third parties on advanced nodes and has already secured its first customers. Success is not guaranteed, however: TSMC has a significant technological lead and even Samsung is struggling to keep up. Intel claims that it is quickly catching up. For example, the Intel 18A process (1.8nm generation) will be accelerated and deployed for new Intel products by the end of the year. If this succeeds, Intel will close the gap with TSMC’s most advanced 2-3nm technology. Geopolitics plays a major role in this. Western governments support Intel with subsidies and government orders to become less dependent on Asian factories. At the same time, export restrictions to China pose a risk: if Intel can’t sell its latest chips freely to Chinese customers, it will limit its addressable market. AMD and NVIDIA are already expecting billions in losses due to stricter US export regulations, a scenario from which Intel is also not immune.

Intel's Market Share Is Faltering, But The Battle Is Not Over

Meanwhile, competition in Intel’s core markets is fierce. In PC CPUs, AMD is nibbling away at Intel’s share with Ryzen chips, although the recent PC market dip hurt both players. In servers, the situation is more dire: AMD’s EPYC processors have gained significant market share, helped by TSMC’s better transistor technology. AMD saw its data center revenue grow 57% in Q1 2025 to $3.7 billion, which is close to the $4.1 billion of Intel’s server and AI division. Intel is still holding on to the biggest customers for now thanks to a trusted ecosystem and its own supply chain, but the trend is clear. In addition, ARM architectures and proprietary chips from cloud players (such as AWS Graviton) are gaining popularity, fragmenting the traditional x86 market. Intel’s technology moat which is based for decades on Moore’s Law and manufacturing dominance has eroded. Still, Intel has strong assets such as deep engineering expertise, its own factories (which are more autonomous in geopolitical conflict) and strong relationships with PC and server OEMs. These moats should help Intel through the transition as the company reinvents itself as a more agile chipmaker.

Intel's Figures Show Recovery, But Remain Fragile

Intel’s recent quarterly results show a company in recovery mode. In Q1 2025, Intel posted revenue of $12.7 billion, virtually the same as Q1 2024. That’s encouraging, as revenue shrank significantly a year ago. Sales of datacenter products clearly picked up, which were driven by demand for Xeon processors at cloud giants. Intel also managed to beat its own expectations: gross margin came in at 39.2% (slightly better than expected) although still well below the 45%+ of a few years ago. Bottom line, Intel reported adjusted earnings per share of $0.13 in Q1. That’s above expectations and shows that cost-cutting and a focus on profitability are starting to take effect. However, GAAP earnings remain in the red with a loss of $0.19 per share, driven by high costs and depreciation.

Intel Tempers Expectations But Cuts Costs Significantly

The short-term outlook remains cautious. Intel itself expects Q2 2025 revenue of $11.2–12.4 billion and net break even profit (non-GAAP). This stagnation is due to continued weakness in the PC market and a wait and see enterprise sector. CFO David Zinsner warned that macroeconomic uncertainty and recession risk will make it difficult to predict the rest of the year. On the positive side, the new CEO is implementing a downsizing operation to make the organization more agile and reduce costs. Intel is reducing the number of management layers and is targeting $17 billion in operating expenses in 2025 (including R&D and SG&A). Intel is also investing selectively: it has reduced its capital expenditures for 2025 to around $18 billion to conserve cash.

Intel Shrinks Mega Plans To Reduce Debt

Some megaprojects have been postponed, such as the construction of a new megafactory in Ohio was pushed back to 2030 until current fab capacity is fully utilized. Intel is also raising cash from non-core assets: the sale of a stake in subsidiary Altera (FPGA division) and the earlier IPO of Mobileye have provided billions to reduce debt. As of the end of Q1, Intel had $21 billion in cash and short-term investments, against a hefty debt load of around $45 billion. The company has started to pay down that debt this year. However, Intel is having to factor in higher interest provisions as borrowing has become more expensive, which is weighing on profit margins for the time being.

In summary, financial performance has been weak by historical standards, profit margins are thin and cash flow is largely consumed by investments, but there are signs of stabilization. If costs continue to decline and revenue recovery continues, Intel could return to sustainable profitability.

Dividends and Valuation

For years, Intel was known for its reliable dividend, but in early 2023, the quarterly dividend was cut by two-thirds to conserve cash. Then in August 2024, Intel even announced that it would pause the dividend completely. And since Q4 2024, shareholders will no longer receive a dividend payment. This drastic decision illustrates the financial necessity: Intel chose to invest in its turnaround over serving dividend investors. The current focus is on debt reduction and capital expenditures, so a dividend recovery does not seem feasible for a few years if the free cash flow improves sufficiently.

On the valuation side, Intel looks cheap at first glance. The P/E ratio is difficult to interpret due to the recent losses (and on a GAAP basis, there is not even a positive profit). Looking at alternative metrics, Intel trades at around 1.6x annual revenue and just 0.9x book value. For comparison, rivals AMD and NVIDIA have much higher multiples.

AMD trades at around 7x revenue, driven by rapid growth, while NVIDIA trades at 23x revenue thanks to its dominant position in AI chips. Intel also fares poorly in terms of margins and returns. While Intel’s gross margin hovers around 40%, AMD’s is above 50% and TSMC’s is around 55%. NVIDIA achieves net profit margins that Intel can only dream of. Intel’s low valuation therefore rightly reflects its current problems.

But this is where the opportunity lies: if Intel can partially regain its old profit levels, the current share price offers a large margin of safety. For example, Intel has historically often traded at 12-15x earnings. With a future recovery to around $3 earnings per share (still a long way off at the moment), the stock should be significantly higher. Of course, that’s not a certainty, Intel could just as easily turn out to be a valuation trap if profitability doesn’t return. The balance sheet is certainly still solid enough to support the transformation, and with government support/subsidies on the way for new factories, Intel can invest on favorable terms. Ultimately, valuation depends on Intel’s execution: can the company execute on the current turnaround and return to growth? If so, the stock looks very cheap; if not, it remains cheap for a reason.

Conclusion

Intel is in the midst of a transformation that carries both risks and potentially huge rewards. For dividend investors, Intel is now little more than a waiting room; income has dried up and will only return once the company is making solid profits again. Value investors, however, see an iconic technology company for sale at bottom prices, the market seems to be pricing in the worst, leaving room for positive surprises. If successful in AI and foundry, Intel could show growth figures in a few years that are not reflected in the price today.

All things considered, I currently consider Intel a Buy. The stock is attractively priced for long-term investors who believe in a gradual recovery. The new CEO is making a fresh start and is focusing on working more efficiently, which is much needed.

My positive view does come with risks. For example, Intel really needs to catch up technologically; a new product backlog or chip production failure could further damage confidence. Competition also remains fierce, and there is no guarantee that Intel will regain market share.

However, Intel has the balance sheet, political support, and know-how to give this turnaround a serious chance of success. Should the turnaround succeed, the returns for buyers at this level could be generous, which is why I find the risk/reward attractive enough to warrant a “Buy rating.” Furthermore, if there are setbacks, the downside potential is limited by the already low valuation. For now, Hold is too conservative given the value unlocked by even moderate success, but Strong Buy is a step too far until Intel provides more evidence of sustained improvement. In short, Intel currently represents an interesting but speculative opportunity for patient investors, and the stock deserves a place on the Buy list for the long term.

data.title: Axcelis: If You're Into Semiconductors, This One's For You
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Proliferation of the semiconductor industry is essential for Axcelis Technologies' (NASDAQ:ACLS) growth along multiple routes. The need for advanced processors, memory devices, and back-end chips, which in turn increases production of the above-mentioned products by the chip manufacturers, brings the qualified ion implantation equipment onboard that Axcelis is an expert in delivering. The trend towards smaller node geometries forms the basis for Axcelis in a growing scenario that adds value beyond mere market expansion. At a fair value of $112.01, I believe the stock offers a robust upside to investors.

In Q1 2025, Axcelis Technologies witnessed a YoY decline with sales plunging from $252.4 million to $192.6 million dollars, indicating a 24% decrease which suggests a downturn in semiconductor equipment requirements. The company cited that non-GAAP the operating margin was being squeezed intensely from 24.1% to 18.3%, revealing that the operational leverage turned out against them as fixed costs did not change much while revenue decreased. The non-GAAP earnings per share were slouched by 38% from $1.68 to $1.04 reflecting the situation of cyclical downturns having a more serious effect on the capital equipment businesses. Nevertheless, the company kept its gross margins just moderate at 46.4% suggesting that pricing discipline is intact, and the product mix is consistent despite pressures on the volume.

Company Overview

Axcelis Technologies is a manufacturer and supplier of ion implantation systems and other related services necessary to the semiconductor industry. The company is best known for the super-precise technology controlling the position of dopant atoms in the silicon wafer used in the transistors. By offering high-current systems for memory devices, medium-current systems for advanced logic chips, and high-energy systems for power semiconductors, Axcelis manages to serve key players in the sector while also being the bridge to several other expanding technology markets.

Key Growth Driver

Gaining Knowledge on Ion Implantation in the Production of Semiconductors

To grasp the fact that Axcelis Technologies is one of the semiconductor companies that directly benefits from the growth of the sector, we should first delve into the technical basis of ion implantation and look at its significance in today's chip manufacturing. Among the most definitive features of ion implantation is that, unlike other methods, it can be fully autonomous, which makes the use case compelling. Ion implantation is not only one of the most automatic and controllable methods, but also the most basic way to create the electronic structures that constitute modern integrated circuits.

The ion implantation process begins with the creation of ionized dopant atoms, typically boron for p-type doping or phosphorus and arsenic for n-type doping, in silicon-based devices. These ions are accelerated through an electric field and directed toward the semiconductor wafer with precisely controlled energy levels. The energy level determines the depth of penetration into the substrate, and the dose indicates the total quantity of the dopant material implanted. Sharp profiles of the doping are critical for the fabrication of the latest transistor structures. Hence, sharp control of the process is paramount.

The semiconductor industry is in a permanent quest for devices with better performance, and that is why advanced doping profiles are necessary to maintain the electrical parameters and keep the reliability of the devices. Miniaturization of the devices to below 10-nanometer logic has meant that the ion implantation machines are now required to be more precise than ever before. The capability of positioning dopants with atomic precision, the uniformity across large wafer areas, and achieving reproducibility from one million devices are the distinct features that make the ion implantation system a critical component in the manufacturing process.

Axcelis Technologies has been particularly focusing on some crucial areas of ion implantation technology. Their high-current implanters are the ones that cope best with the challenges of memory device manufacturing, where the main target is large wafer volumes, and therefore they are invaluable. The medium-current systems they have are the key to the very precisions intended for the development of logic devices, and high-energy implanters are there to build deep junctions in the power with semiconductor devices. This technical versatility enables Axcelis to be present in various sectors of the ever-growing semiconductor market.

New Technology Nodes and Equipment Needs

The semiconductor sector is in a ceaseless quest for shriveling down the geometries of transistors and enhancing the performance characteristics, which in turn generates a relentless need for more advanced manufacturing equipment. This cycle of technology advancement is the prime factor for the growth of Axcelis Technologies, considering the fact that every new generation of semiconductor devices requires better ion implantation capabilities.

Switching from planar transistor constructions to streamlining the architecture with advanced 3D structures like FinFETs and gate-all-around transistors has vastly added to the difficulties in ion-implantation processes. These systems require the entailed programming of several steps with different angles and energies to dope all the device regions. The accuracy requirements for these processes are beyond the scope of older generations of machines. Thus, experts are called in to run the advanced systems, such as those provided by Axcelis.

The new wave of computing power is mainly made from novel memory technologies, including 3D NAND flash and next-generation DRAM architectures, necessitating changes in the specialized ion implantation processes that the industry has carried to the limit of the current technology. 3D NAND devices, for instance, demand the careful insertion of vertical channel structures that go across several layers of material. This process poses a requirement for ion implantation devices that are capable of sustaining uniformity along the high-aspect-ratio features while, at the same time, delivering the exact dopant profiles that the device needs to function.

The entry of new semiconductor materials beyond the prevalent silicon technology has opened up yet another avenue for specialized ion-impactors. Gallium arsenide and gallium nitride devices that are intended for high-frequency and power applications have different implanting parameters, unlike silicon devices. The high energy, silicon carbide power devices require implantation processes with high-energy that only certain machines are able to carry out. Axcelis has been creating knowledge in these recently available material systems, thus laying a bedrock for the company to grow with the upsurge in the industry. An increase in the complexity of processes has effectively made ion implantation one of the major constraints in the semiconductor manufacturing process. Modern devices may require multiple implantation steps, each step specifically designed for beam current, energy, dose, and uniformity, among others.

Valuation - DCF Model

The WACC of 11.25% in my calculations is in line with 10.51% assigned by Prof. Aswath Damodaran for the semiconductor equipment industry. This manifests how the market perceives this industry at large relatively riskier, primarily due to its nascency.

Axcelis is undervalued with a fair value of $112.01 representing an upside potential of 65.7%. The model deploys fairly conservative projections, albeit consistent with Axcelis' past median financial performances. For instance, Axcelis' revenue grew at a median rate of 38.3% over the past five years, but it would be absolutely unreasonable to expect Axcelis' revenue to grow at that rate. Hence, the model deploys a moderate median rate of 10%.

The Competitive Scene

The largest ion implantation system supplier is Applied Materials, greatly benefiting it from the largest semiconductor equipment supplier. In addition to ion implantation, this company also brings large financial assets, a wide range of collaborations, and extensive research and development capabilities to the area. The integrated equipment solutions and all-inclusive service bundles existing Applied Materials can offer are the main strength of the company, which is not reachable by smaller companies like Axcelis.

Specificity Advantage: Depth Outperforms Breadth

The primary edge of Axcelis against Applied Materials lies in its single-minded concentration on ion implantation technology. Applied Materials, due to their diverse linear product line, cannot allocate engineering resources only on deposition systems, etching equipment, or metrology tools which are the types of semiconductor equipment they use most often, but must distribute them on a total of several dozen machines. On the other hand, Axcelis channels almost all of its technological capabilities into improving and perfecting only one process - ion implantation.

This strategy introduces what economists refer to as "economies of specialization". The specific focus of Axcelis on ion implantation is the key to the competitive edge that Applied Materials is unable to replicate even among its range of products. Every engineering hour, research dollar, and operational improvement at Axcelis contributes to deeper expertise in ion implantation technology. This concentrated learning generates compound returns on knowledge investment that exceed what Applied Materials can achieve by spreading similar resources across multiple technology areas.

The Edge over Ion implantation

Ion beam transport system layout necessitates a thorough comprehension of charged particle optics, magnetic focusing effects, and space charge compensation mechanisms that Axcelis has especially optimized for ion implantation applications. The beam line structures of the company are manifestations of the long-term specialized advancement in ion optics, which Applied Materials is not capable of duplicating without the same kind of investments.

The beam transport systems of Applied Materials are bound to the considerations of the company's larger equipment portfolio, which can lead to a suboptimal configuration for specific ion implantation requirements. The compromises in beam line design required for multi-product manufacturing efficiency result in the level of ion implantation that Axcelis, with its straight focus, would achieve that is not accessible in this case.

Key Risks

Cyclical Fluctuations in Market Volatility and Demand Variability

The main risk that Axcelis faces and which is the most considerable and immediate risk is the factor due to its own cyclic nature of the semiconductor industry, which results in such primary demand swings that can impact the company's financial performance in a significant way. Semiconductor markets have yet a reason to follow the cycle of boom and bust due to factors such as inventory adjustments, technology transitions, and macroeconomic factors that lead to a state of only a limited time intense equipment demand followed by only a limited time dramatic slowdowns.

The service and support business is a very important buffer for cyclical volatility. In the case of new equipment sales, which go to zero in downturns, the situation is different because customers are still using their old equipment and need spare parts, maintenance, and process support. Even in this scenario, Axcelis has strategically incorporated this cash flow model into its operations to ensure that it has money available during the periods when the equipment market is experiencing price drops. Service revenue, on the other hand, usually has a much smaller reduction compared to equipment sales, so it still helps to keep the financial health of the company when the new orders are not there.

Recommendation

Ion implantation has become a part of every semiconductor manufacturing business nowadays. The competitive position of Axcelis is thus anchored in its technical foundation in the crucial ion implantation factor. As semiconductor manufacturers face newer challenges like the increase of such devices and stricter performance standards, the high-tech state-of-the-art ion implantation tools are ever more indispensable. A potential upside of 65% in ACLS stock is surely a hyper-growth opportunity that investors would not want to miss.

data.title: Wise: Attractive Long-Term Growth Runway
data.description:
Investment action

I give a buy rating for Wise plc (OTCPK:WPLCF) as I believe the business has a very attractive long-term growth runway, driven by a solid moat that gives structural cost advantage, an effective customer acquisition channel due to its word-of-mouth-driven marketing, and a long-term growth catalyst (Wise Platform). As such, I think the risk-reward here is very attractive for long-term investors.

Company description

WPLCF is a fintech company that helps facilitate international money transfers with its own payment infrastructure instead of relying on traditional banking networks. Its main products include Wise Account for individuals, Wise Business for small and medium-sized companies [SMBs], and Wise Platform, which lets banks and large enterprises offer WPLCF services under their own brand.

FY25 Earnings Review

WPLCF did extremely well for FY25, with active customers growing by 21% y/y to 15.6 million, which drove a 23% y/y increase in cross-border volume to GBP145.2 billion (25% on a constant currency basis). That, and the 33% y/y growth in Wise Accounts customer balance to GBP21.6 billion, translated into GBP1.36 billion of underlying income, which was up 16% y/y. But the most impressive part of the results, the way I see it, is margin expansion. Despite the WPLCF initiative to lower the cross-border take rate to just 53 bps in 4Q25 to encourage adoption, they managed to expand gross margins from 73% to 75%, which shows that the business still has good operating leverage. This strong operating leverage flowed straight through to profits, which drove underlying profit before taxes [PBT] up by 17% y/y to GBP282 million, or a 21% margin. Given this performance, I think the FY26 guidance of 15–20% income growth and a 13–16% PBT margin are well within reach. In fact, it may be conservative if WPLCF continues this level of execution.

Multiple Levers to Drive Share Gains and Long-Term Growth

I believe WPLCF has a really long runway of growth ahead, and a major reason is because of the massive addressable market and room for market share gains. Despite WPLCF's scale today, it has really only captured <5% of the personal cross-border transfer market and <1% of the SMB segment. In absolute terms, the entire market is sized at GBP32 trillion. WPLCF has demonstrated its ability to capture share, and this can be seen from WPLFC already handling 10% of all personal remittance inflows to India and 12% to the Philippines. Some may think that this is due to WPLCF's aggressive pricing strategy, and it is not sustainable over the long term. I disagree. I believe WPLCF is able to pursue an aggressive pricing model because of its proprietary infrastructure. In other words, WPLCF has a structural cost advantage against competitors that rely on traditional banking networks.

Over the years, WPLCF has invested a lot to make this work, and I believe it has become a strong moat that peers cannot replicate easily. For example, a recent direct connection into the Philippines enabled a 8x reduction in transaction costs. If you had to pay $1 for each transfer previously, it now costs you $0.125. This is a massive cost savings that matters. This cost advantage allows WPLCF to undercut on pricing, capture volume, and drive up gross profits, which gives it even more capital to invest in its direct payment networks. Essentially, the result is a virtuous flywheel.

WPLCF's proprietary network is not the only reason it is able to continue winning share. It also has a very efficient customer acquisition model. For reference, >70% of new users come through word-of-mouth, which is basically free and also reflects the brand equity among users (users wouldn’t refer this if the brand were safe and reliable). This organic acquisition strategy reduces acquisition costs, which allows it to invest more aggressively in R&D and its networks, which I see as another structural cost advantage vs. peers that rely on heavy marketing spend to acquire customers.

Aside from the consumer and SMB opportunity, one long-term catalyst that could really steepen the growth curve is Wise Platform. The platform goal is to help facilitate international transfers for large institutions like Morgan Stanley, Standard Chartered, and Itaú. These large institutions have relied on their legacy systems, which are more expensive than Wise Platform. If WPLCF can position itself as a solution to lower costs for these institutions, I think the upside is huge. Management sees this segment growing from 3-4% of revenue today to 10% in the medium term, and potentially as much as 50% over the long run. The numbers look better at the bottom line because this revenue stream has very little incremental cost, given that it leverages existing payment volumes and requires minimal incremental sales effort.

US Listing Provides a Clear Catalyst for Re-Rating

One interesting move that management has made was to shift WPLCF's primary listing to the US. I understand this has nothing to do with the fundamentals, but from an equity investor's point of view, I think there is a lot of value in doing so. By listing in the US, it broadens the potential investor base by opening the stock up to US institutional and retail investors who may currently avoid or be restricted from investing in UK-listed equities. In simple terms, it significantly enhances trading liquidity. In addition, this opens up the pathway for WPLCF to be included in major US indices such as the S&P 500 or Russell benchmarks.

Valuation

The addressable market is large, and the business has already shown its ability to capture share. With its structural moat and effective customer acquisition model, I believe WPLCF can continue to grow at very attractive rates for the next few years. I modeled 18% growth CAGR through FY28. Margins should slowly creep up as WPLCF scales up, driving in operating leverage. By FY28, I expect WPLCF to generate GBP727 million in net income. I did not model in an upwards re-rating in multiples, since I do not expect growth to accelerate. But I like to point out that Remitly Global (RELY) trades at 35x forward PE with a similar growth outlook vs. WPLCF today, so that gives precedence that WPLCF could see its multiple go up if the market enters a bull market.

Risk

WPLCF operates in a highly regulated environment, and while the track record so far is good, if WPLCF were to incur any compliance lapse, it could erode customer trust and heavily impact its word-of-mouth marketing. In addition, if adoption of the Wise Platform fails to meet expectations, it would temper the long-term growth narrative and delay any potential valuation re-rating.

Conclusion

I give a buy rating for WPLCF because of the attractive growth runway, and it has demonstrated it can grow efficiently. The upcoming US listing should also help re-rate the stock by broadening its investor base and unlocking index inclusion over time. With a strong moat and multi-year growth runway, I think the stock offers attractive upside.

data.title: Tariff Dent To U.S. Inflation Limited In May, Deeper Impact Expected Ahead
data.description:
Expected price hikes from a wave of US tariffs on global trade have yet to meaningfully show up in government inflation data, but economists warn that increases may be close.

The consumer price index, the market's preferred inflation metric, increased 2.4% from May 2024 to May 2025, essentially unchanged since March, the US Bureau of Labor Statistics reported June 11. The core index, which removes volatile energy and food prices, increased 2.8% from May 2024, the smallest annual increase since May 2021.

Prices of all items tracked within the index increased just 0.1% from April to May, matching the average monthly growth since February and showing little impact of the historic increase in tariffs from the Trump administration.

"The tariff impacts are buried in the details of the report, but admittedly, their visibility in May CPI is lower and narrower across the various categories than we and most other economists expected," said Oren Klachkin, a financial market economist at Nationwide. "We expect to see a stronger and broader pass-through in the summer."

Klachkin said some subcategories within the government inflation data, such as audio equipment and household furnishings, which increased 1.6% and 0.3%, respectively, from April, showed some tariff impacts.

Some goods with the highest exposure to Chinese supply chains saw a relatively substantial increase. Prices for toys, for example, increased 1.3% from April. But other categories, expected to be hit hard by tariffs, such as motor vehicles and clothing, saw little to no impact. Prices for new vehicles, for example, fell by 0.3% from April, while apparel fell 0.4%.

"If we examine the sequential inflation increase in categories like toys, appliances, and computers, it suggests that some companies may have managed to implement price increases, but not enough to significantly affect the index," said Julien Lafargue, chief market strategist at Barclays Private Bank. "The impact of tariffs, if any, will become clearer over time, but we do not anticipate a surge in inflation, especially if more deals are made."

Consumers were at least partly shielded from the tariffs, since businesses were still working off inventories built up in anticipation of President Trump's plans to upend US trade policy and could be holding off on passing on price increases.

"Corporate America took huge steps to mitigate tariff risk by stockpiling inventory in late 2024 and early this year," said David Russell, global head of market strategy at TradeStation. "We're more likely to see the costs trickle through and old deals that used to be available disappear."

Summer climb

Relatively low prices are unlikely to last much longer.

"Some businesses will absorb the tariffs to protect their market share, but others will pass them onto customers, and some firms will use a mix of both approaches," Klachkin with Nationwide said.

The relatively limited impact of tariffs on inflation data so far may be a poor reflection of what is to come, said James Knightley, chief international economist with ING.

"Markets obviously like this, but we need to be careful of saying that tariff fears are overdone," Knightley said.

In the Federal Reserve's Beige Book for May, a summary of economic commentary from contacts throughout the US that the central bank released on June 4, tariffs were mentioned 122 times. Tariffs were mentioned just 23 times in the Fed's January Beige Book and not mentioned at all in the October release, prior to Trump's election victory.

"There were widespread reports of contacts expecting costs and prices to rise at a faster rate going forward," the latest Beige Book states. "All district reports indicated that higher tariff rates were putting upward pressure on costs and prices."

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Meta's AI Will Kill Agencies And Capture The Spoils
data.description:
I’ve been a permabull on Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) (TSX:META:CA) over the past five years, and the stock is by far my largest position. So it should come as no surprise that I feel super bullish about everything that's going on in the AI space and how it can tangibly improve the business, both top and bottom-line-wise.

As you know, we are no longer in the phase of glossy promises about AI. Meta has been reaping the benefits of AI for quite some time, with management often pointing out how all the progress over the years has helped boost key metrics, keeping users more engaged and pushing ad revenue higher. CEO Mark Zuckerberg, in fact, expressed in Meta's most recent earnings call that "AI is transforming everything we do."

However, this appears to be getting pretty serious at this point, as META is building a platform that could turn every small business into its own ad agency, slash creative costs, and fatten margins like never before. None of these factors seems to have been priced into today's valuation, which still leaves room for significant upside, in my view.

Today’s AI Play: The Ad Machine Already Humming

Meta’s current AI setup is already a beast, quietly powering the ads that fuel 97% of its $160 billion (and growing) annual revenue. If you scroll through Instagram or Facebook, you’ll likely be hit with ads that know you too well, as I am sure you've noticed. Meta’s AI is at work here, as it leverages 3.43 billion daily active users to deliver hyper-targeted ads with surgical precision.

For context, Meta's Advantage+ campaigns, which the company launched back in 2023, utilize AI to optimize ad placements across platforms. This has since led to notably higher ROAS for advertisers. Small businesses, in particular, the backbone of Meta’s 10+ million advertisers, have been the main beneficiaries, as they can literally create campaigns on the fly without needing to hire someone who would previously be assigned such a task.

Now, beyond targeting, Meta’s AI already generates ad creatives (images, text, even basic videos), once again saving advertisers time and cash. Take WhatsApp’s business messaging, for instance, where you already have AI chatbots handling millions of customer queries, tapping into budgets once reserved for CRM giants like Salesforce (CRM).

The 2026 Vision: Total Creative Control in AI’s Hands

Now, here's where this is going. By the end of 2026, the company plans to fully automate ad creation and targeting, as reported last week. This is the era where your local bakery uploads a cupcake photo and, with a $500 budget, has Meta’s AI generate a full-blown campaign, including videos, captions, and targeting tailored to local foodies, all in real-time. By shifting creative control into the hands of Meta’s algorithms and sidelining traditional ad agencies, both Meta and advertisers should be able to win on a tremendous level at the expense of the middlemen.

Even a decade ago, it wouldn’t have been far-fetched to imagine a future like this. It was clear that the long-term vision for Meta would ultimately center on a powerful AI system automating everything and optimizing outcomes for the company, advertisers, and users alike. So it's thus easy to see why Meta is all-in on this with a $64–72 billion CapEx budget this year, part of which is to go build the AI infrastructure needed.

Beyond the top-line benefit for Meta coming off of this trend, this shift could be a margin goldmine. By automating creative production, Meta eliminates labor-intensive processes, which should, in turn, allow campaigns to scale without incurring ballooning costs. So we are potentially talking about a big structural shift in how advertising dollars flow.

The Big Idea: Redefining Creative Control

Another element within this potentially huge structural shift is the ownership of the creative process. Today, brands rely on agencies (the middleman, as I mentioned earlier) for the “art” of advertising. This includes anything from storytelling to visuals and brand voice. Meta’s AI threatens (at least from the point of view of the middleman) to democratize that, giving businesses direct control via algorithms that learn their style and audience.

With regard to that, Mr. Zuckerberg said the following in his opening statements during Meta's Q1 earnings call:

Businesses used to have to generate their own ad creative and define what audiences they wanted to reach. But AI has already made us better at targeting and finding the audiences that will be interested in their product than many businesses are themselves, and that keeps improving. And now AI is generating better creative options for many businesses as well. I think that this is really redefining what advertising is into an AI agent that delivers measurable business results at scale.

The reason this may be massive is that, beyond saving dollars on the medium-sized businesses, small businesses, which so far have not been able to afford agencies, suddenly become their own creative directors. This means that a whole new class of advertisers will now be able to allocate even relatively new dollars to Meta. And even if their budgets are undersized, medium, and large advertisers may have to increase their bids to "recapture" the lost attention, ultimately driving ad prices higher.

The Valuation Remains Attractive, As Always

In the meantime, Meta's valuation is still attractive. The market doesn't seem to price any explosive benefit from the company's AI developments, but that doesn't surprise me, as the stock has historically been mispriced. If you are a long-term META bull, you know that the stock is likely to trade at a discount, but you are still betting on the fact that the underlying growth is going to drive significant gains, even if valuation multiples don't expand.

But what truly baffles me these days is not only Meta's attractive valuation, but the fact that analysts' estimates seem entirely out of touch. Wall Street sees a mere 7% growth in EPS this year, which is a total joke of an estimate, given that Meta delivered EPS growth of 37% in Q1 (following last year's full-year EPS growth of 60%!).

Wall Street's medium-term EPS growth forecasts, which hover in the mid-teens, also make little to no sense. Meta's 5-year trailing-12-month revenue and EPS CAGR stand at 18.4% and 29.5%, while the company is most likely entering a multi-year cycle of AI-powered top-line acceleration and margin expansion. The fact that Wall Street "denies" boosting their estimates is also being reflected in the fact that EPS beats have become consistently wider in recent quarters.

Another way to prove this point is that even after Meta beating EPS estimates by 23.5% in Q1, the consensus EPS estimate for the full year has remained unchanged, which, I think, is very obvious to say that it's mathematically unfit.

In turn, looking at Meta's forward multiples makes little sense. They are based on outdated (to say the least) forecasts that not only fail to capture Meta's current momentum, but also don't even dare to account for any outsized AI-driven benefit. Thus, I remain comfortably long, and averaging up on my position from time to time.

Risks

Now, despite Meta’s AI-driven strategy delivering results, thus far, it doesn't come without risks. First, the substantial capital expenditure means that Meta has to keep growing at strong rates to justify its investment. So far, its return on capital has been excellent, and even strengthened lately. Still, if the AI tailwind were to be disrupted, Meta could face strong investor scrutiny.

Then, you have regulatory scrutiny around data privacy and AI ethics. In the future, we may find out that Meta is not allowed to fully leverage user data for ad targeting. This could potentially impact revenue growth. And of course, competition in the AI space is intensifying, with Google and Amazon also investing heavily, which could, in turn, challenge Meta’s market dominance, especially if their investments in AI pay off better.

Conclusion

I am very excited about Meta's prospects moving forward. Sure, there are some risks worth keeping in mind, but today's reality is that Meta is leveraging AI to redefine advertising by empowering businesses with automated, cost-efficient tools that drive engagement and margins.

And let's not forget that Meta has a solid track record of topping expectations and a valuation that, if anything, seems to underestimate its AI-fueled growth potential. So, yes, it may seem scary to chase the stock above the $700 threshold, but given how attractive the stock's forward multiples are, today's share price levels may look far behind us in the near future. As with all winners, I always say it's smart to start a position, and gradually dollar-cost-average your way through the ups and the downs. This has been my strategy of accumulating Meta stock over the past five years, and thus far, it has worked out nicely.

data.title: ISD: A Decent Way To Get Junk Bond Exposure If The Price Improves
data.description:
The PGIM High Yield Bond Fund (NYSE:ISD) is a closed-end fund that invests in junk bonds and similar assets with the goal of providing its investors with a very high level of income. This is a fairly popular investment proposition right now due to the fact that many retirees need more income than Social Security alone provides. In addition, the rapid increase in prices that we have seen over the past five years has left many individuals seeking additional money that they can use to supplement their salaries. As such, there could certainly be many reasons for investors to seek out income opportunities in the current market. The PGIM High Yield Bond Fund would appear to be such a solution.

The PGIM High Yield Bond Fund certainly does fairly well in terms of the provision of income. The fund currently has a 9.11% yield, which is substantially higher than that of just about any domestic fixed-income index. We can see that quite clearly in this comparison table:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

3.85%

6.62%

6.67%

As we can clearly see, the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund has a significantly higher yield than either domestic junk bonds or domestic preferred stocks. As many income-seeking investors have the explicit goal of trying to maximize their incomes, this is a potential selling point for this fund.

It is, however, worth noting that the current yield of the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund is not particularly impressive when compared to that of its peers. This can be clearly seen in this chart:

The PGIM High Yield Bond Fund is not the absolute lowest fund shown here in terms of yield, but it is fairly close. There are nine funds shown on the chart, and only one of them – the BNY Mellon High Yield Strategies Fund – has a lower yield than the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund. This might reduce the appeal of this fund in the eyes of many investors since, after all, an investment in this fund will generate significantly less income than the same amount of money invested in most of the fund’s peers. However, the low yield relative to the fund’s peers is a vote of confidence from the market that this fund can sustain its distribution going forward.

The fund’s recent performance does give us reason to believe that it will be able to sustain the distribution. This chart shows the fund’s net asset value year-to-date:

As we can see, the net asset value of the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund has risen by 0.58% since the start of the year. This is a much better performance than some other closed-end bond funds have delivered. For example, as was mentioned in an article that I published on this site yesterday, the PIMCO High Income Fund (PHK) has actually seen its net asset value decline over the same period. This means that the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund has delivered a much better performance year-to-date than some others, which any investor should appreciate. The fact that its net asset value increased also means that this fund has covered all of the distributions that it has paid out since the start of the year, supporting the previous conclusion that this fund should be able to sustain its distribution and provide an income to its shareholders.

Recent Performance Update

We last discussed the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund on November 16, 2023. As such, quite a lot of time has passed since our last discussion. In fact, it has been approximately nineteen months, and a lot of things can happen during that period in the financial markets. These most recent nineteen months are certainly no exception.

This chart shows the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index, which tracks domestic junk bonds, from November 16, 2023, through today:

As we can see, the index has risen by 5.04% over the period, which suggests that yields are down overall. However, we can see that there was certainly some volatility here, as junk bond yields rose (the index declined) during first few weeks of April 2024, and the same thing happened even more severely in the first few weeks of April 2025 following the American imposition of tariffs on much of the rest of the world. However, for the most part, junk bonds did better than investment-grade bonds:

We can see that the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, which tracks domestic investment-grade bonds, only rose 2.69% over the period. Perhaps the more important thing to note, though, is that bond yields rose substantially during the final quarter of 2024. This was particularly the case for the longest-dated bonds, as shown in this table:
