The yield of the ten-year U.S. Treasury is down slightly from the start of the year, although it is still higher than was the case back on September 30, 2024. However, the yields on both the twenty-year and the thirty-year U.S. Treasury bonds are higher than was the case at the start of the year. Once again, this increase in long-term yields is something that occurred despite the Federal Reserve giving no signs that it intends to start raising interest rates and despite the fact that inflation is actually down since the start of this year:

Overall, in terms of monetary policy, the fundamentals today are actually looking a lot better than they were back when the market was aggressively projecting rate cuts over the summer of 2024. That would normally point to falling bond yields, not rising ones.

A recent article in the Washington Post provides some insight into what seems to be occurring within the bond markets. From that article:

Investors are warning that governments can’t keep borrowing at the pace they did when interest rates were close to zero, particularly since trade tensions and sticky inflation have diminished the probability that policymakers will dramatically ease monetary policy. A pullback by central banks and pension funds from bond markets also means a once-dependable source of funding is increasingly absent.

In other words, investors are beginning to question the long-term fiscal viability of the United States and other developed countries. Many of these countries have very high unfunded liabilities due to aging populations, and bond market investors are unsure how governments will ever be able to repay their obligations, short of printing money and destroying the value of their currencies. This also explains why gold, platinum, and silver have all been rising lately, as investors are starting to trust precious metals as a store of value rather than fiat currencies and bonds.

However, as the above charts show, junk bonds have not been as vulnerable to rising long-term rates as investment-grade bonds have been. In fact, the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index is up slightly year-to-date:

One of the reasons for this could be that junk bonds tend to have relatively short durations. The Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index has an option-adjusted duration of only 3.01 years right now:

The PGIM High Yield Bond Fund has a leverage-adjusted duration of 4.0 years, so not much longer than that of the index. This means that investors in the bonds included in the fund will end up getting all of their money back in only a few years, which is a very different scenario than what long-term government bonds deliver. The steepening yield curve seems to suggest that the market thinks that the status quo will be able to continue for at least the next five to ten years, so they are comfortable lending fiat currency for that period of time without demanding extra yield. As such, this fund should be at least somewhat insulated from the problems that funds holding longer-term bonds face.

As junk bonds have been relatively insulated from the problems in the long-term bond market and domestic junk bonds are actually up since our last discussion, we might expect the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund to have also delivered a fairly respectable performance over the period. This is indeed the case, as shares of this fund are up a whopping 18.00% since my previous article was published:

As we can clearly see, the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund managed to outperform both the domestic investment-grade and the domestic junk bond indices by a considerable margin. This is somewhat unsurprising, as we would ordinarily expect a leveraged bond fund to deliver greater gains than the indices during a period of rising prices, but this is still a phenomenal performance over the period.

Naturally, investors in this fund did even better on a total return basis. We can see that quite clearly in this chart:

As shown, investors in the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund benefited from a 36.00% total return over the period in question. This is far better than either the 8.93% total return that domestic investment-grade bonds delivered, and it is even better than the 16.71% total return that domestic junk bonds provided to investors. Overall, any investor in this fund is likely to be quite pleased with this performance since, after all, everybody likes to do better than the comparable indices. If a fund cannot at least beat the index, then there is no real point in paying for active management.

About The Fund

The website for the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund states that the fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a very high level of current income. The website also states that the fund aims to achieve this objective by investing in below-investment-grade securities:

The fund’s objective makes a great deal of sense given that strategy. After all, the primary way in which high-yield bonds deliver their investment returns is through direct payments to their investors. In fact, just like all bonds, these securities provide no net capital gains when held from issuance until maturity. We can see this by comparing the price performance to the total return performance of the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index over time. This chart shows that comparison:

As we can see, the SPDR Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF, which is an exchange-traded index fund that tracks the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index, delivered a 129.56% total return since it first started trading on November 28, 2007. However, the share price actually declined by a whopping 33.12% since its inception. This means that literally all of the total investment return that investors received from this index over that entire period came from the coupon payments made by the bonds in the index. These coupon payments are a form of income, so this reinforces the fact that junk bonds are income vehicles. Thus, this fund’s objective makes a great deal of sense.

As regular readers can attest, it is not unusual for us to see a junk bond fund with exposure to things other than traditional junk bonds. For example, floating-rate bank loans (leveraged loans) are a relatively common holding, and many junk bond funds even have a small allocation to common stocks. As is the case with other closed-end bond funds, we do see exposure here to floating-rate securities and common stocks. However, these non-traditional bond assets account for less than 20% of the fund’s portfolio, so they do not have much of an impact on its overall performance. Traditional junk bonds still account for most of the fund’s portfolio, and it is the performance of this security type that will have the greatest impact on the fund’s own share price performance. With that said, though, we should not ignore the fact that this fund has exposure to other asset classes. It is not only invested in junk bonds like the index is.

In particular, the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund has quite a bit of exposure to various kinds of floating-rate securities. The fund’s exposure to floating rate securities is more than just the assets listed in the “Floating Rate and Other Loans” category. For starters, all of the asset-backed securities held in the fund’s portfolio are floating-rate collateralized loan obligations:

We can clearly see that all of these securities include an explicit statement that says that they are linked to the 3-Month Secured Overnight Financing Rate. This immediately identifies them as floating-rate securities. In addition to these securities, the exchange-traded fund listed in the fund’s schedule of investments is the PGIM AAA CLO ETF (PAAA), which invests in highly rated collateralized loan obligations.

The PGIM AAA CLO ETF holds assets that also have rates that vary with some underlying interest rate benchmark, such as the Secured Overnight Financing Rate. As such, these are also floating-rate securities. The asset-backed securities, floating-rate loans, and the exchange-traded fund account for 15.7% of the fund’s net assets. This is, admittedly, not as high as the exposure that some other junk bond funds have to floating-rate assets, but it is still high enough for these floating-rate securities to account for a notable proportion of the fund’s net investment income.

As was shown earlier in this article, inflation has been declining since the start of this year. The latest inflation report shows that this is largely due to oil prices remaining in the $60 to $70 per barrel range, as opposed to the much higher energy prices that we had back in 2022 through 2024, but either way, the headline data says that inflation has come down since the start of the year. This has prompted many market watchers to wonder when the Federal Reserve will resume its previously stated plan to reduce interest rates. After all, despite the claims that the imposition of tariffs will cause prices to rise, there are no signs that inflation is actually any more of a problem than it was back in late 2024 when the central bank cut rates by 100 basis points. In short, with this latest inflation report, the market has increased the probability that the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates by a total of 50 basis points in 2025. As of right now, the federal funds futures market says that there is a 40.9% chance of two 25-basis point cuts by the end of this year:

Please recall that in an article written yesterday, I showed that at the time, the market thought that there was a 31.8% probability of one 25-basis point cut, a 39.0% probability of two 25-basis point cuts, and an 18.2% probability of three 25-basis point cuts by year-end. Today, likely as a direct result of the tame inflation report, the probabilities for both two and three 25-basis point cuts have increased, while the probability that there will only be one 25-basis point cut has decreased. Thus, the market thinks that the Federal Reserve will be more aggressive with rate cuts than previously thought due to inflation continuing to be cooler than what many economists believe.

This will naturally have an impact on the overall performance and net investment income of the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund. First of all, any interest rate cuts will reduce the income that the fund receives from the collateralized debt obligations and the floating-rate loans included in the fund’s portfolio. While these securities only account for a minority of the fund’s asset base, it still represents a reduction in net investment income and a reduction in the amount of money that the fund could conceivably pay out to its investors. This might be offset (at least temporarily) by the junk bonds in the portfolio rising in price. There is no guarantee that this will happen, though, due to the broader problems in the bond market. As we discussed earlier, some bonds have actually been experiencing rising yields despite the Federal Reserve’s attempts to lower interest rates. So far, junk bonds have been more resilient to this than long-duration U.S. Treasuries, but there is no guarantee that this will always be the case since investors will always demand extra interest for holding junk bonds as opposed to U.S. Treasuries.

Distribution Analysis

The primary objective of the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund is to provide its investors with a high level of current income. In accordance with this objective, the fund pays a monthly distribution of $0.1050 per share ($1.26 per share annually), which gives it a 9.11% yield at the current price. The fund has been maintaining this distribution since 2019, so it has been quite consistent over the past few years.

As we saw earlier in this article, the fund’s net asset value has increased year-to-date, which suggests that it has been able to afford the distributions that it has paid out since December 31, 2024. Let us take a look at the fund’s finances so we can get a better picture, however.

As of the time of writing, the most recent financial report that is available for the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund is the semi-annual report for the six-month period that ended on January 31, 2025. A link to this report was provided earlier in this article. Let us consult the financial section of it now.

For the six-month period that ended on January 31, 2025, the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund reported a net investment income of $15,495,952. This was not sufficient to cover the $20,951,736 that the fund paid out in distributions to its shareholders over the period.

Fortunately, this fund was able to make up for the difference through other methods. For the six-month period that ended January 31, 2025, the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund reported net realized losses of $11,926,664, but this was fully offset by $21,524,001 of net unrealized gains. The fund also received $7,824 in capital gains distributions from another PGIM fund. Overall, these things combined with the fund’s net investment income increased its assets by $25,101,113 over the period:

This was sufficient to cover the distribution, but the fund had to use its unrealized capital gains to ensure full coverage of the distribution. This can be somewhat unsafe due to the fact that unrealized gains can be erased by the market, but as we saw earlier, this fund has done fairly well at covering its distributions year-to-date, so it does not appear that it has given up its unrealized capital gains yet.

Valuation

Shares of the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund are currently trading at a 0.86% discount on net asset value. This is reasonably in line with the 0.65% discount that the fund’s shares have had on average over the past month.

Historically, however, this fund has traded at a substantially larger discount than it has today. Over the past three years, shares of the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund have traded at prices ranging from a 14.84% discount to a 3.22% premium on net asset value. The average price over the three-year period was a 7.19% discount, which is substantially larger than the fund’s discount today. Thus, it is possible that mean reversion will occur at some point in the future and drive the fund’s share price down a bit from its present levels.

Expenses

The PGIM High Yield Bond Fund has an expense ratio of 2.53%, which is higher than most index funds, but we expected it to be. After all, this fund employs leverage as a part of its strategy and so it has to pay interest on this borrowed money. If we exclude these interest expenses, the fund has an expense ratio of 1.09%:

This is, overall, not really too bad for an actively managed junk bond fund.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund appears to be a reasonably solid junk bond fund, although it does have some exposure to the floating-rate securities market. This floating-rate exposure will likely cause the fund’s net investment income to decline in the near future, as it is very difficult to believe that the Federal Reserve will not cut interest rates later this year, considering how tame inflation has been. It is possible, but by no means certain, that interest rate cuts will push up the price of junk bonds as well as this fund’s share price. Unfortunately, this is probably unlikely because the market has already priced in the expected rate cuts to the pricing of the fund’s assets, and this fund’s short duration means that interest rate cuts will not move the price of its bonds very much anyway.

Personally, I am inclined to remain cautious with respect to any bond fund given the troubles with U.S. Treasuries, but this one does seem like a decent way to get exposure to junk bonds if the discount widens.
